_**User (2025-11-04 18:35Z)**_

this is a collaborative doc viewing and editing project in team size of 2 members here is the complete overview of the project: The system is composed of the following core components:
    User Clients:
        Represent the users interacting with the system.
        Provide the interface for performing operations on files (create, view, read, write, delete, etc.).
        Multiple clients may run concurrently, and all must be able to interact with the system simultaneously.
    Name Server:
        Acts as the central coordinator of the system.
        Handles all communication between clients and storage servers.
        Maintains the mapping between file names and their storage locations.
        Ensures efficient and correct access to files across the system.
    Storage Servers:
        Responsible for storing and retrieving file data.
        Ensure durability, persistence, and efficient access to files.
        Support concurrent access by multiple clients, including both reads and writes.

At any point, there would be a single instance of the Name Server running, to which multiple instances of Storage Servers and User Clients can connect. The User Clients and Storage Servers can disconnect and reconnect at any time, and the system should handle these events gracefully.

The event of Name Server failure is out of scope for this project. That is, if the Name Server goes down, the entire system is considered down and must be restarted.
The File

Files are the fundamental units of data in the system, each uniquely identified by a name. Files are restricted to text data only. Every file consists of multiple sentences, and each sentence is made up of words. A sentence is defined as a sequence of words ending with a period (.), exclamation mark (!), or question mark (?). Words within a sentence are separated by spaces. This segmentation needs to be handled by the system, the user should be able to access the file as a whole. For completeness and in interest of no ambiguity, a word is defined as a sequence of ASCII characters without spaces.

There is no imposed limit on file size or the total number of files, so the system must efficiently handle both small and large documents (which can variably grow after creation also).

Files support concurrent access for both reading and writing. However, when a user edits a sentence, that sentence is locked for editing by others until the operation is complete. This allows multiple users to view or edit the file simultaneously, but prevents simultaneous edits to the same sentence.
[150] User Functionalities

The users (clients) must be able to perform the following operations:

    [10] View files: User can view all files they have access to. They can view all files on the system, irrespective of the access using “-a” flag. “-l” flag should list files along with details like word count, character count, last access, owner, etc. Note, a combination of flags can be used lke “-al”, which should list all the files with details.

    VIEW  # Lists all files user has access to
    VIEW -a # Lists all files on the system
    VIEW -l # Lists all user-access files with details
    VIEW -al # Lists all system files with details

    [10] Read a File: Users can retrieve the contents of files stored within the system. This fundamental operation grants users access to the information they seek.

    READ <filename> # Prints the content of the complete file

    [10] Create a File: Users can create new files, allowing them to store and manage their data effectively.

    CREATE <filename> # Creates an empty file with name <filename>

    [30] Write to a File: Users can update the content of the file at a word level. This operation allows users to modify and append data to existing files.

WRITE <filename> <sentence_number>  # Locks the sentence for other users (if no file write access, should return appropriate here)
<word_index> <content> # Updates the sentence at <word_index> with <content>
.
.
.
<word_index> <content> # User can update the sentence multiple times
ETIRW # Relieves the sentence lock, allowing other users to finally write

Few important points to note here:

    After each WRITE completion, the sentence index update. So, care must be taken for ensuring concurrent WRITEs are handled correctly.
    The content may contain characters like period (.), exclamation mark (!), or question mark (?). The system should be able to recognise these sentence delimiters and create seperate sentences accordingly. (Please refer to examples given below, for more clarity).
        Yes, every period (or question / exclamation mark) is a sentence delimiter, even if it is in the middle of a word like “e.g.” or “Umm… ackchually!”

    Hint: For resolving concurrent read-write issues, you may write to a temporary swap file initially, and move the contents to the final file once all updates are complete. You may also consider using locks, semaphores, some algorithmic approach, etc.

    [15] Undo Change: Users can rever the last changes made to a file.

    UNDO <filename> # Reverts the last change made to the file

Note: The undo-es are file specific, and not user specific. So, if user A makes a change, and user B wants to undo it, user B can also do it. The undo history is maintained by the storage server.

    [10] Get Additional Information: Users can access a wealth of supplementary information about specific files. This includes details such as file size, access rights, timestamps, and other metadata, providing users with comprehensive insights into the files they interact with.

    INFO <filename> # Display details in any convenient format, just that all above-mentioned details should be there

    [10] Delete a File: Owners should be able to remove files from the system when they are no longer needed, contributing to efficient space management. All data like user access should be accordingly updated to reflect this change.

    DELETE <filename> # Deletes the file <filename>

    [15] Stream Content: The client establishes direct connection with the Storage Server and fetches & displays the content word-by-word with a delay of 0.1 seconds between each word. This simulates a streaming effect, allowing users to experience the content in a dynamic manner.

    STREAM <filename> # Streams the content of the file word by word with a delay of 0.1 seconds

Note: If the storage server goes down mid-streaming, an appropriate error message should be displayed to the user.

    [10] List Users: Users can view a list of all users registered in the system.

    LIST # Lists all users in the system

    [15] Access: The creator (owner) of the file can provide access to other users. The owner can provide read or write access. The owner can also remove access from other users. The owner always has both read and write access.

    ADDACCESS -R <filename> <username>  # Adds read access to the user
    ADDACCESS -W <filename> <username>  # Adds write (and read) access to the user
    REMACCESS <filename> <username>  # Removes all access

    [15] Executable File: Users (with read access) can “execute” the file. Execute, here, means executing the file content as shell commands. The output of the command should be displayed to the user.

    EXEC <filename> # Executes the file content as shell commands

Note: The execution must happen on the name server; and the outputs as is should be piped to the client interface
[40] System Requirements

The system must support the following requirements:

    [10] Data Persistence: All files and their associated metadata (like access control lists) must be stored persistently. This ensures that data remains intact and accessible even after Storage Servers restart or fail.

    [5] Access Control: The system must enforce access control policies, ensuring that only authorized users can read or write to files based on the permissions set by the file owner.

    [5] Logging: Implement a logging mechanism where the NM and SS records every request, acknowledgment and response. Additionally, the NM should display (print in terminal) relevant messages indicating the status and outcome of each operation. This bookkeeping ensures traceability and aids in debugging and system monitoring. Each entry should include relevant information such as timestamps, IP, port, usernames and other important operation details crucial for diagnosing and troubleshooting issues.

    [5] Error Handling: The system must provide clear and informative error messages for all sorts of expected / unexpected failures, including interactions between clients, Name Server (NM), and Storage Servers (SS). Define a comprehensive set of error codes to cover scenarios such as unauthorized access, file not found, resource contention (e.g., file locked for writing) and system failures. These error codes should be universal throughout the system.

    [15] Efficient Search: The Name Server should implement efficient search algorithms to quickly locate files based on their names or other metadata, minimizing latency in file access operations. Furthermore, caching should be implemented for recent searches to expedite subsequent requests for the same data.

    Note: An approach faster than O(N) time complexity is expected here. Efficient data structures like Tries, Hashmaps, etc. can be used.

[10] Specifications
1. Initialisation

    Name Server (NM): The first step is to initialize the Naming Server, which serves as the central coordination point in the NFS. It is responsible for managing the essential information about file locations and content.

    Note: The IP address and port of the Naming Server can be assumed to be known publicly so that it can be provided to Clients and Storage servers while registering.

    Storage Server (SS): Each Storage Server is responsible for physically storing the files and interacting with the Naming Server.Upon initialization, the SS sends vital details about its existence to the Naming Server. This information includes: IP address, port for NM connection, port for client connection and a list of files on it.

    Client: Clients on initialisation should ask the user for their username (for file accesses) and pass this information along with its IP, NM port and SS port to the Name Server.

2. Name Server

    Storing Storage Server data: One of the fundamental functions of the NM is to serve as the central repository for critical information provided by Storage Servers (SS) upon connection. This information is maintained by NM, to later direct data requests to appropriate storage server. As mentioned in specification 2, these lookups need to be efficient.
    Client task feedback: Upon completion of tasks initiated by clients, the NM plays a pivotal role in providing timely and relevant feedback to the requesting clients. This is really important in real-systems where client response latency is pivotal.

3. Storage Servers

The Storage Servers are equipped with the following functionalities:

    Adding new storage servers: New Storage Servers (i.e., which begin running after the initial initialisation phase) have the capability to dynamically add their entries to the NM at any point during execution. This flexibility ensures that the system can adapt to changes and scaling requirements seamlessly. The initialisation process at the storage server side follows the same protocol as described in Specification 1.
    Commands Issued by NM: The Name Server can issue specific commands to the Storage Servers, such as creating, editing or deleting files. The Storage Servers are responsible for executing these commands as directed by the NM.
    Client Interactions: Some operations require the client to establish direct connection with the storage server. The storage server is expected to facilitate these interactions as needed.

4. Client

Whenever a client boots up, it asks the user for their username. This username is then used for all file access control operations. The system should ensure that users can only perform actions on files they have permissions for, based on their username. This username is relayed to the NM, which stores it along with the client information until the client disconnects.

Clients initiate communication with the NM to interact with the system. Here’s how this interaction unfolds:

    Any file access request from the client is first sent to the NM, which locates the corresponding Storage Server hosting that file (one of many), using its locally stored information.
    Depending on the type of operation requested by the client, the NM may either handle the request as a middleman or facilitate direct communication between the client and the appropriate Storage Server. The operations can be broadly categorized as follows:
        Reading, Writing, Streaming : The NM identifies the correct Storage Server and returns the precise IP address and client port for that SS to the client. Subsequently, the client directly communicates with the designated SS. This direct communication is established, and the client continuously receives information packets from the SS until a predefined “STOP” packet is sent or a specified condition for task completion is met. The “STOP” packet serves as a signal to conclude the operation.
        Listing files, Basic Info and Access Control : The NM handles these requests directly. It processes the client’s request and retrieves the necessary information from its local storage. Once the information is gathered, the NM sends it back to the client, providing the requested details without involving any Storage Server.
        Creating and Deleting Files : The NM determines the appropriate SS and forwards the request to the appropriate SS for execution. The SS processes the request and performs the specified action, such as creating / deleting the file. After successful execution, the SS sends an acknowledgment (ACK) to the NM to confirm task completion. The NM, in turn, conveys this information back to the client, providing feedback on the task’s status.
        Execute : The NM requests for information from SS, but the main processing and communication is handled by the NM directly. The NM executes the commands contained within the file and captures the output. This output is then relayed back to the client, providing them with the results of the executed commands. HERE ARE A FEW EXAMPLES: Examples

    Note: There is no specification on the exact format of the commands. The commands mentioned in the examples are indicative. You may choose to implement them in any format you like, as long as the functionality remains the same.

Client: WRITE mouse.txt 0 # Adding to the start of file

ERROR: Sentence index out of range.  # Similarly for word indexes (negative or > number of words + 1, should results in errors)

Client: 3 T-T
Client: ETIRW
Write Successful!

Im just a mouse. I dont like T-T PNS

Client: 4 deeply mistaken hollow lil gei-fwen # New sentence : Im just a deeply mistaken hollow lil gei-fwen pocket-sized mouse.
Client: 6 pocket-sized  # New sentence : Im just a deeply mistaken hollow pocket-sized lil gei-fwen mouse.
Client: ETIRW

Im just a deeply mistaken hollow pocket-sized lil gei-fwen mouse. I dont like T-T PNS

Client: 5 and AAD. aaaah # New sentences : [I dont like T-T PNS and AAD.]* [aaaah]. Currently active status remains with the index at index 1
Client: 0 But,  # New sentence : [But, I dont like T-T PNS and AAD.]* [aaaah].
Client: ETIRW
Write Successful!

Client: READ mouse.txt
Im just a deeply mistaken hollow pocket-sized lil gei-fwen mouse. But, I dont like T-T PNS and AAD. aaaah.

The multiples writes within a single WRITE call, are all considered a single operation. Note this while implementing features like UNDO

Proper error handling should cover:

    Attempting to write without access
    Attempting to write a locked sentence
    Invalid indices
    Updates are applied in order received, so later updates operate on the already modified sentence.

Example 5: Undo Change

Client: READ nuh uh.txt
rm -rf /  # Oops!

Client: WRITE nuh_uh.txt 0
Client: 0 sudo
Client: ETIRW
Write Successful!

Client: READ nuh_uh.txt
sudo rm -rf /

Client: UNDO nuh_uh.txt
Undo Successful!

Client: READ nuh_uh.txt
rm -rf /

Note:

    Only one undo operation for a file needs to be supported. Multiple undos are beyond the scope of this project.
    Undo operates at the Storage Server level and only reverts the most recent change.
    If the current user is user1 and the most recent modification to the file was made by user2, then an UNDO command issued by user1 should revert user2’s last change.

_**Agent (model copilot/claude-sonnet-4.5)**_

Great! I understand this is a distributed file system project with a Name Server, Storage Servers, and Clients. As the **Data Plane Developer**, you're responsible for building:

1. **User Client** - Interface for users to interact with the system
2. **Storage Server** - Handles actual file storage and retrieval

Let me break down your work and provide a starting roadmap:

## **Your Responsibilities Overview**

### **Storage Server (SS):**
- Store and retrieve files physically
- Handle concurrent read/write operations with sentence-level locking
- Implement UNDO functionality (track last change per file)
- Support STREAM operation (word-by-word delivery)
- Communicate with both Name Server and Clients
- Ensure data persistence across restarts

### **User Client:**
- Provide CLI interface for all user commands
- Register with Name Server (send username, IP, ports)
- Route requests appropriately (some via NM, some direct to SS)
- Handle responses and display formatted output

## **How to Start: Step-by-Step Approach**

### **Phase 1: Project Setup & Basic Architecture**
1. **Choose your language** - C/C++ is typical for such projects (network programming with sockets)
2. **Create project structure:**
   ```
   /client
      - client.c/cpp (main client logic)
      - client_nm_comm.c/cpp (communication with Name Server)
      - client_ss_comm.c/cpp (direct communication with Storage Server)
      - command_parser.c/cpp (parse user commands)
   /storage_server
      - storage_server.c/cpp (main SS logic)
      - ss_nm_comm.c/cpp (communication with Name Server)
      - ss_client_comm.c/cpp (handle client requests)
      - file_handler.c/cpp (CRUD operations on files)
      - lock_manager.c/cpp (sentence-level locking)
      - persistence.c/cpp (save/load metadata)
   /common
      - protocol.h (shared message formats, error codes)
      - network_utils.c/cpp (socket helpers)
   ```

### **Phase 2: Start with Storage Server Core**

**Step 1: Basic SS Initialization**
- Create a server that listens on two ports:
  - One for Name Server connections
  - One for Client connections
- Implement registration with NS (send IP, ports, file list)

**Step 2: File Operations Foundation**
- Implement basic file operations:
  - CREATE file
  - DELETE file
  - READ file (return complete content)
  - Store files in a designated directory
- Design metadata structure (owner, timestamps, access control list, word/char counts)

**Step 3: WRITE Operation with Locking**
- Parse sentences (split by `.`, `!`, `?`)
- Implement sentence-level locking mechanism
- Handle word insertion at specific indices
- Handle sentence splitting when delimiters are inserted
- This is the most complex operation!

**Step 4: UNDO Implementation**
- Store previous version of file before each write
- Implement single-level undo per file

**Step 5: STREAM Operation**
- Send file content word-by-word with 0.1s delay
- Handle connection drops gracefully

**Step 6: Persistence**
- Save file metadata to disk
- Implement recovery on restart

### **Phase 3: Build User Client**

**Step 1: Client Initialization**
- Prompt for username
- Connect to Name Server and register (username, IP, ports)

**Step 2: Command Parser**
- Parse all commands: VIEW, READ, CREATE, WRITE, DELETE, INFO, STREAM, LIST, ADDACCESS, REMACCESS, EXEC
- Validate syntax and arguments

**Step 3: Client-NM Communication**
- Send requests to Name Server
- Handle responses for metadata operations (VIEW, LIST, INFO, ACCESS control)

**Step 4: Client-SS Direct Communication**
- For READ: Get SS details from NM, connect to SS, receive file
- For WRITE: Get SS details, connect, send WRITE commands, handle locking
- For STREAM: Connect to SS, display words with 0.1s delay

**Step 5: User Interface**
- Format output nicely (tables for VIEW -l)
- Display appropriate error messages
- Handle edge cases

### **Phase 4: Protocol Design**

You need to define:
1. **Message formats** between Client-NM, Client-SS, SS-NM
2. **Error codes** (file not found, access denied, sentence locked, etc.)
3. **ACK/NACK protocols**

Example protocol structure:
```c
struct Message {
    int type;  // REQUEST, RESPONSE, ACK, ERROR
    int operation;  // CREATE, READ, WRITE, DELETE, etc.
    char username[256];
    char filename[256];
    int sentence_index;
    int word_index;
    char data[MAX_SIZE];
    int error_code;
};
```

### **Critical Implementation Details**

1. **Concurrent Access:**
   - Use mutexes/semaphores for sentence locking
   - Multiple readers OK, but only one writer per sentence
   - Reader-writer locks might be useful

2. **WRITE Operation Logic:**
   ```
   - Lock sentence
   - Parse current sentence into words
   - Insert/update words at specified indices
   - Check for sentence delimiters in new content
   - Split into multiple sentences if needed
   - Update file
   - Release lock
   ```

3. **Data Persistence:**
   - Store files in filesystem
   - Maintain metadata file (JSON or binary format)
   - Save: owners, timestamps, access lists, undo history

## **Immediate Next Steps:**

1. **Set up your development environment** (compiler, IDE)
2. **Start with a simple Storage Server** that:
   - Listens on a port
   - Can accept connections
   - Can CREATE and READ files
3. **Build a basic Client** that:
   - Can connect to SS
   - Can send CREATE and READ commands
4. **Test basic flow** before adding complexity
5. **Gradually add features** (WRITE, locking, UNDO, etc.)

_**User (2025-11-04 19:03Z)**_

Message Structure (The struct)

This is a good, practical design for this project. Using a single struct is a common and simple way to handle C-based network communication.

Suggested Changes:

    Ambiguous Port Fields: Your client_port and nm_port are ambiguous because both the Client and the SS send different port information during registration.

        SS sends: "port for NM connection" and "port for client connection".

Client sends: "NM port" and "SS port".

    Suggestion: Rename client_port to port1 and nm_port to port2. This way, you can reuse them:

        For OP_SS_REGISTER: port1 is the SS's port for NM comms, port2 is the SS's port for Client comms.

        For OP_CLIENT_REGISTER: port1 is the Client's port for NM comms, port2 is the Client's port for SS comms.

Generic IP Field: Rename client_ip[MAX_IP_LEN] to ip[MAX_IP_LEN], since the SS also sends its IP address during registration.

SS File List: The SS must send a "list of files on it" when it registers. Your data[MAX_DATA_SIZE] field is the logical place for this. You just need to be aware that 4096 bytes might not be enough for a very long list. For this project, assuming the list will fit in MAX_DATA_SIZE is likely a valid "necessary assumption".

Here is a revised struct based on these suggestions:
C

#define MAX_FILENAME 256
#define MAX_USERNAME 64
#define MAX_DATA_SIZE 4096
#define MAX_IP_LEN 16

typedef struct {
    int msg_type;        // MSG_REQUEST, MSG_RESPONSE, etc.
    int operation;     // OP_CREATE, OP_READ, etc.
    int error_code;    // ERR_FILE_NOT_FOUND, etc.

    char username[MAX_USERNAME];
    char filename[MAX_FILENAME];
    
    // For WRITE
    int sentence_index;
    int word_index;

    // For Registration
    char ip[MAX_IP_LEN]; 
    int port1; // Renamed from client_port
    int port2; // Renamed from nm_port

    // For WRITE content, READ response, file lists, etc.
    char data[MAX_DATA_SIZE]; 
} Message;  Rest all is fine

_**Agent (model copilot/claude-sonnet-4.5)**_

Yes, I have implemented the WRITE functionality! Let me explain how it works in detail.

## **WRITE Implementation Overview**

The WRITE operation is one of the most complex features. Here's how it works:

### **1. Client Side (`client_ss_comm.c` - `send_write_request` function)** Let me continue reading: ### **2. Storage Server Side (`ss_client_comm.c` - `handle_write_request` function)** ### **3. The Core File Manipulation (`file_handler.c` - `write_to_file` function)** ## **Complete WRITE Flow Explanation**

Here's how the WRITE operation works step-by-step:

### **Step 1: User Issues WRITE Command**
```bash
Client> WRITE myfile.txt 1
```
- Client parses this and extracts filename (`myfile.txt`) and sentence index (`1`)

### **Step 2: Get Storage Server Info**
- Client asks Name Server: "Which Storage Server has `myfile.txt`?"
- Name Server responds with SS IP and port

### **Step 3: Connect to Storage Server**
- Client connects directly to the Storage Server

### **Step 4: Lock the Sentence**
- Client sends WRITE request with filename and sentence_index
- SS checks write access permissions
- SS attempts to lock sentence using `lock_sentence()` (thread-safe with mutexes)
- If locked by another user → Error returned
- If successful → Lock acquired, SS sends ACK with "LOCKED"

### **Step 5: Save Undo Backup**
- Before any modifications, SS saves the current file content to `undo/filename.undo`
- This enables the UNDO functionality

### **Step 6: Interactive Write Commands**
User enters multiple write commands:
```
> 0 Hello
> 3 beautiful
> ETIRW
```

For **each command**, the `write_to_file()` function:

1. **Reads the entire file** into memory
2. **Splits into sentences** by finding `.`, `!`, `?` delimiters
3. **Validates sentence_index** (must be ≤ sentence_count)
4. **Gets target sentence** (the one being edited)
5. **Splits sentence into words** (by spaces)
6. **Validates word_index** (must be ≤ word_count + 1)
7. **Inserts new content** at word_index:
   - Shifts existing words to make room
   - Places new content at specified position
8. **Checks for sentence delimiters** in new content:
   - If content contains `.`, `!`, or `?` → Splits into multiple sentences
   - Example: Inserting "AAD." creates a new sentence
9. **Reconstructs the file**:
   - Sentences before target
   - Modified sentence(s)
   - Sentences after target
10. **Writes back to disk**
11. **Updates metadata** (word count, char count, modified time)

### **Step 7: ETIRW - Release Lock**
- Client sends "ETIRW" message
- SS unlocks the sentence using `unlock_sentence()`
- SS sends final success response

### **Key Features Implemented:**

✅ **Sentence-level locking** - Only one user can edit a sentence at a time
✅ **Word-level insertion** - Insert at any word position (0 to word_count)
✅ **Sentence delimiter handling** - Automatically splits when `.`, `!`, `?` detected
✅ **Concurrent writes** - Different users can edit different sentences simultaneously
✅ **UNDO support** - Backup saved before modifications
✅ **Access control** - Only users with write permission can edit
✅ **Error handling** - Invalid indices, locked sentences, access denied

### **Example from Project Spec:**

```
File: "Im just a mouse."

WRITE mouse.txt 1
> 3 deeply mistaken hollow lil gei-fwen
> 6 pocket-sized
> ETIRW

Result: "Im just a deeply mistaken hollow pocket-sized lil gei-fwen mouse."
```

**How it works:**
1. Sentence 1: "Im just a mouse."
2. Split into words: ["Im", "just", "a", "mouse."]
3. Insert at position 3: ["Im", "just", "a", "deeply", "mistaken", "hollow", "lil", "gei-fwen", "mouse."]
4. Insert at position 6 (in updated sentence): ["Im", "just", "a", "deeply", "mistaken", "hollow", "pocket-sized", "lil", "gei-fwen", "mouse."]
5. Reconstruct and write back

This implementation handles **all the complex cases** mentioned in the project specification including multiple insertions, sentence delimiter detection, and concurrent access!

_**User (2025-11-04 19:30Z)**_

so how have you structured the entire file and given word indices and sentence indices to all the data? linked list/array/anything else? what is the data structure you are using?

---

_**Agent (model copilot/claude-sonnet-4.5)**_

Great question! Let me explain the data structure approach I used for the file handling.

## **Data Structure: Simple In-Memory Arrays (Not Persistent)**

I'm **NOT** using linked lists or complex data structures. Instead, I use a **simple, straightforward approach**:

### **The Core Approach:**

**Files are stored as plain text on disk**, and when we need to modify them:

1. **Read entire file into memory** as a string
2. **Parse on-the-fly** into temporary arrays
3. **Manipulate the arrays**
4. **Write back as plain text**

Let me show you the exact implementation: Perfect! Now let me explain the data structures clearly:

## **Data Structures Used:**

### **1. File Storage on Disk**
```
storage_server/
    files/
        myfile.txt          <- Plain text: "Hello world. This is fun!"
        anotherfile.txt     <- Plain text content
    undo/
        myfile.txt.undo     <- Backup for undo
    metadata.txt            <- Binary file with metadata structs
```

**Files are stored as plain text** - no special formatting, no indices stored.

### **2. In-Memory Processing (Temporary Arrays)**

When a WRITE operation happens, the function creates **temporary stack-allocated arrays**:

```c
// Step 1: Read entire file into string
char file_content[MAX_DATA_SIZE];  // Single string: "Hello world. This is fun!"

// Step 2: Parse into sentence array
char sentences[1000][MAX_DATA_SIZE];  // 2D array of sentences
int sentence_count = 0;

// sentences[0] = "Hello world."
// sentences[1] = "This is fun!"
// sentence_count = 2
```

**Sentence Indices**: Just array indices (0, 1, 2, ...)
- `sentences[0]` = first sentence
- `sentences[1]` = second sentence

```c
// Step 3: Parse target sentence into word array
char words[1000][256];  // 2D array of words
int word_count = 0;

// For sentence "Hello world."
// words[0] = "Hello"
// words[1] = "world."
// word_count = 2
```

**Word Indices**: Just array indices (0, 1, 2, ...)
- `words[0]` = first word
- `words[1]` = second word

### **3. Manipulation**

```c
// Insert at word_index (e.g., word_index = 1)
// Shift words to make room
for (int i = word_count; i > word_index; i--) {
    strcpy(words[i], words[i - 1]);
}
strcpy(words[word_index], content);  // Insert new content
word_count++;
```

After inserting "beautiful" at index 1:
```
words[0] = "Hello"
words[1] = "beautiful"    <- newly inserted
words[2] = "world."       <- shifted
word_count = 3
```

### **4. Reconstruction**

```c
// Reconstruct sentence from words array
char new_sentence[MAX_DATA_SIZE] = "";
for (int i = 0; i < word_count; i++) {
    if (i > 0) strcat(new_sentence, " ");
    strcat(new_sentence, words[i]);
}
// Result: "Hello beautiful world."
```

```c
// Reconstruct entire file from sentences array
char final_content[MAX_DATA_SIZE] = "";

// Add sentences before target
for (int i = 0; i < sentence_index && i < sentence_count; i++) {
    if (i > 0) strcat(final_content, " ");
    strcat(final_content, sentences[i]);
}

// Add modified sentence(s)
strcat(final_content, new_sentence);

// Add sentences after target
for (int i = sentence_index + 1; i < sentence_count; i++) {
    strcat(final_content, " ");
    strcat(final_content, sentences[i]);
}
```

### **5. Write Back to Disk**

```c
// Write final content back as plain text
fp = fopen(filepath, "w");
fwrite(final_content, 1, strlen(final_content), fp);
fclose(fp);
```

## **Summary of Data Structures:**

| Component | Data Structure | Persistence |
|-----------|---------------|-------------|
| **File content** | Plain text string on disk | Persistent |
| **Sentences** | `char sentences[1000][MAX_DATA_SIZE]` | Temporary (during operation only) |
| **Words** | `char words[1000][256]` | Temporary (during operation only) |
| **Metadata** | `FileMetadata metadata_list[MAX_FILES]` | Persistent (saved to metadata.txt) |
| **Sentence locks** | `SentenceLock locks[MAX_LOCKED_FILES]` | In-memory only (lost on restart) |

## **Why This Approach?**

### **Advantages:**
✅ **Simple** - Easy to understand and debug
✅ **No complex memory management** - Stack-allocated arrays
✅ **Works with plain text files** - Can edit with any text editor
✅ **Straightforward parsing** - Standard C string functions
✅ **No fragmentation issues** - Complete rewrite each time

### **Disadvantages:**
❌ **Not efficient for large files** - Loads entire file into memory
❌ **O(n) complexity** - Must parse entire file each time
❌ **Fixed size limits** - `MAX_DATA_SIZE = 4096 bytes`
❌ **Memory usage** - Temporary arrays use significant stack space

## **Sentence/Word Indexing is Dynamic:**

**Important**: Indices are **computed on-the-fly** every time we access the file:

```
File content: "Hello world. This is fun!"

Each WRITE operation:
1. Reads file
2. Parses into sentences → assigns indices 0, 1, 2...
3. Parses sentence into words → assigns indices 0, 1, 2...
4. Modifies
5. Writes back

Next WRITE operation:
1. Reads file (with modifications)
2. Re-parses (indices may change if content changed!)
3. ...
```

This is why the project spec says: "After each WRITE completion, the sentence index update" - because indices are recalculated from the text each time!

**Would you like me to show you an alternative approach using linked lists or a more efficient data structure?**

_**User (2025-11-04 19:32Z)**_

will your implementation work for a file having little less than 1 lakh lines with each line having atleast 50 words?

---

_**Agent (model copilot/claude-sonnet-4.5)**_

**No, my current implementation will NOT work for such large files.**

Let me analyze why:

## **Problem Analysis:**

### **Your File Size:**
- **~100,000 lines** (1 lakh ≈ 100,000)
- **~50 words per line**
- Assuming ~5 characters per word + spaces
- **~300 characters per line**
- **Total: 100,000 × 300 = 30,000,000 bytes = ~30 MB**

### **Current Implementation Limitations:** ```c
#define MAX_DATA_SIZE 4096  // Only 4 KB!
```

## **Critical Failures:**

### **1. Buffer Overflow in `write_to_file`:**
```c
char file_content[MAX_DATA_SIZE];  // 4096 bytes
size_t bytes_read = fread(file_content, 1, MAX_DATA_SIZE - 1, fp);
```
**Your file: 30 MB** → Will only read first 4 KB!

### **2. Array Size Limits:**
```c
char sentences[1000][MAX_DATA_SIZE];  // Max 1000 sentences
```
**Your file: 100,000 lines** → Will overflow after 1000 sentences!

### **3. Network Message Size:**
```c
typedef struct {
    ...
    char data[MAX_DATA_SIZE];  // Only 4096 bytes per message
} Message;
```
**Cannot send entire file content in one message!**

### **4. Stack Overflow:**
```c
char sentences[1000][MAX_DATA_SIZE];  // 1000 × 4096 = ~4 MB on stack!
char words[1000][256];                 // 1000 × 256 = ~256 KB on stack!
char final_content[MAX_DATA_SIZE];    // 4096 bytes
```
**Total stack usage: ~4.5 MB per write operation** → Stack overflow risk!

### **5. Memory & Performance:**
- Reading/writing entire 30 MB file for every single word insertion = **extremely inefficient**
- Parsing 100,000 lines on every WRITE = **very slow**

## **What Will Actually Happen:**

1. **File creation works** (empty file)
2. **First few writes work** (while file is small)
3. **Once file grows beyond 4 KB:**
   - READ operations will only return first 4 KB
   - WRITE operations will **corrupt the file** (only reading first 4 KB, losing rest)
   - File content beyond 4 KB will be **lost**
4. **If somehow you bypass the 4KB limit:**
   - Array overflow when sentence count > 1000
   - **Segmentation fault / crash**

## **Solutions:**

### **Option 1: Increase Limits (Quick Fix - Partial Solution)**

Increase buffer sizes, but still has fundamental issues:

```c
#define MAX_DATA_SIZE (50 * 1024 * 1024)  // 50 MB
#define MAX_SENTENCES 200000               // 200k sentences
```

**Problems:**
- Still loads entire file into memory
- Very slow for large files
- High memory usage

### **Option 2: Streaming/Chunked Processing (Better)**

Read and process file in chunks:
- Read file line-by-line or in blocks
- Only load the target sentence into memory
- Use temporary file for modifications
- More complex but scalable

### **Option 3: Database/Index-Based (Best for Large Files)**

Use a proper data structure:
- Build an index of sentence positions in file
- Seek directly to target sentence
- Modify only that portion
- Update index

### **Option 4: Hybrid Approach (Recommended)**

For this project, I suggest:

_**Agent (model copilot/claude-sonnet-4.5)**_

Excellent! You're absolutely right. Let me implement a proper data structure using linked lists. Before I start coding, let me confirm the design with you:

## **Proposed Linked List Structure:**

```c
// Word node - stores a single word
typedef struct WordNode {
    char word[256];              // The actual word
    struct WordNode *next;       // Next word in the sentence
} WordNode;

// Sentence node - stores a sentence (linked list of words)
typedef struct SentenceNode {
    WordNode *words_head;        // Head of word linked list
    char delimiter;              // '.', '!', '?' or '\0' for last sentence
    struct SentenceNode *next;   // Next sentence
} SentenceNode;

// File structure - represents entire file in memory
typedef struct FileStructure {
    SentenceNode *sentences_head; // Head of sentence linked list
    int sentence_count;
    char filename[MAX_FILENAME];
} FileStructure;
```

## **Visual Representation:**

```
File: "Hello world. This is fun!"

FileStructure
    |
    v
[SentenceNode 1] -> [SentenceNode 2] -> NULL
    |                   |
    delimiter='.'       delimiter='!'
    |                   |
    v                   v
[Word:"Hello"] -> [Word:"world"] -> NULL
                  
                  [Word:"This"] -> [Word:"is"] -> [Word:"fun"] -> NULL
```

## **Write Operation Flow:**

1. **Load file** → Parse into linked list structure
2. **Traverse to target sentence** (using sentence_index)
3. **Traverse to target word** (using word_index) in that sentence
4. **Insert new word(s)** at position
5. **Check for delimiters** in inserted content → may create new sentences
6. **Update pointers** accordingly
7. **Write to temporary swap file** by traversing the entire structure
8. **Replace original file** with swap file using `rename()`

## **Questions Before Implementation:**

- **Option A**: Load entire file into linked list at server startup, keep in memory, sync to disk on changes
- **Option B**: Load on-demand (parse when WRITE starts, free after completion)

Swap:     "myfile.txt.tmp" or ".myfile.txt.swp"
```
Which naming convention?

- **Option A**: Still load entire file into linked list (memory intensive but simpler)
- **Option B**: Use a hybrid approach where we only load the affected sentence and nearby context

### **4. Concurrent Writes:**

- Both load file into linked list
- Both modify their respective parts
- Who writes first wins, second one's changes might overwrite?

_**User (2025-11-04 19:47Z)**_

1. Memory Management Strategy

I strongly recommend you change your suggestion to Option A (Load file into memory and keep it).

Your suggestion (Option B, on-demand) will be very slow and will likely fail to handle concurrency.

    Problem with Option B (On-Demand): Imagine two clients WRITE to the same file at the same time.

        Client 1 loads file.txt (Version 1) into its memory.

        Client 2 loads file.txt (Version 1) into its memory.

        Client 1 modifies sentence 5 and saves file.txt (Version 2).

        Client 2 modifies sentence 8 and saves file.txt (Version 3).

        Result: Client 2's save (Version 3) completely overwrites Client 1's change. Client 1's edit is lost.

    Solution with Option A (Keep in Memory):

        The Storage Server has one linked list for file.txt in its memory.

        Client 1's request comes in → The server locks sentence 5 of that single list.

        Client 2's request comes in → The server locks sentence 8 of that same list.

        Both clients can edit at the same time because the spec allows it.

## **Revised Design - Option A (Keep in Memory):**

### **Server Maintains In-Memory Representation:**
```c
// Global hash table or array to store loaded files
typedef struct {
    char filename[MAX_FILENAME];
    SentenceNode *sentences_head;
    int sentence_count;
    pthread_rwlock_t file_lock;     // Reader-writer lock for file-level access
    int is_loaded;                   // Flag to indicate if file is in memory
} LoadedFile;

LoadedFile loaded_files[MAX_FILES];  // Keep all files in memory
```

### **Flow:**

1. **Server Startup**: Load all existing files from disk into linked list structures
2. **File Access**: All operations (READ/WRITE) work on the in-memory linked list
3. **Sentence Locking**: Lock specific sentence nodes for WRITE operations
4. **Persistence**: Periodically sync to disk (or after each WRITE completion)
5. **Concurrent Writes**: Different sentences can be edited simultaneously on the same in-memory structure

### **Concurrency Handling:**
```
Single in-memory linked list for file.txt:
[Sent0] -> [Sent1] -> [Sent2] -> ... -> [Sent8] -> ...

Client 1: WRITE file.txt 5
  → Locks Sent5 in the shared structure
  → Modifies words in Sent5
  → Unlocks Sent5
  → Syncs to disk

Client 2: WRITE file.txt 8 (concurrent)
  → Locks Sent8 in the same shared structure
  → Modifies words in Sent8
  → Unlocks Sent8
  → Syncs to disk
```

Both clients work on the **same in-memory structure**, so no changes are lost!

When Client 1 saves, the server writes the entire in-memory list (containing both changes) to disk.

This is the only practical way to manage the sentence-level locking and concurrent access requirements.

2. Swap File Naming

Confirmed. Either convention is fine.

myfile.txt.tmp is very clear and common. This is a good "necessary assumption" to make.

3. Handling Large Files

You should use Option A (Load entire file).

While this is memory-intensive, Option B (Hybrid) is far too complex for this project. The complexity of managing partial file loads, disk seeks, and in-memory merges will be a huge time sink.

For this project, it is a reasonable trade-off to choose the simpler (Option A) solution to get the functionality working, even if it has memory limitations for "infinitely" large files.

4. Concurrent Writes (Locking)

You must NOT use file-level locking.

The project specification requires sentence-level locking.

    "...when a user edits a sentence, that sentence is locked for editing by others... This allows multiple users to... edit the file simultaneously" .

Using a file-level lock would block all other users and violate the "simultaneously" requirement.

Correct Locking Strategy (Tied to Decision #1):

    You must use Option A (Keep in Memory).

    Your SentenceNode struct should contain a pthread_mutex_t.

    When a client sends WRITE <filename> <sentence_number>..., your server thread:

        Finds the in-memory FileStructure.

        Traverses to the SentenceNode for <sentence_number>.

        Calls pthread_mutex_lock() on that sentence's mutex.

        If another user has it locked, the WRITE command fails with ERR_SENTENCE_LOCKED.

    When the client sends ETIRW:

        The server performs the final modifications.

        It saves the entire in-memory structure to disk (using the swap file method).

        It calls pthread_mutex_unlock() on the sentence's mutex.

5. Sentence Delimiter Handling

It should just be one sentence ending with "."

The spec says the system recognizes delimiters "and create[s] seperate sentences accordingly".

    Your Scenario: I like T-T PNS + AAD.

    Result: The linked list for the current sentence is appended with the new word AAD.. The SentenceNode's delimiter field is updated to .. It is still one sentence.

It would only become two sentences if the user's content was, for example, AAD. Oh no, as shown in the project example. In that case:

    AAD. would be appended to the current sentence.

    A new SentenceNode would be created.

    Oh and no would be WordNodes in that new sentence. any doubt-ask me directly

proceed with option B: Lazy Loading (Option B)

Con: It is more complex. To prevent the race condition, you must implement a "loader lock."

Implementation: When a thread needs a file that isn't in memory, it must:

    Acquire a global "File Cache Mutex".

    Check again if the file is in memory (in case another thread loaded it while it was waiting for the mutex).

    If it's still not there, load the file from disk, parse it, and add it to the cache.

    Release the "File Cache Mutex".

    Proceed with the operation (e.g., locking the sentence).

I've successfully implemented **backup and replication functionality** for your distributed file system! Here's what was accomplished:

### 🎯 What Was Built

1. **Backup Architecture**
   - Odd-numbered servers (SS1, SS3, SS5) = PRIMARY
   - Even-numbered servers (SS2, SS4, SS6) = BACKUP
   - SS2 backs up SS1, SS4 backs up SS3, etc.

2. **New Components**

   - Updated protocol with 4 new operations (OP_BACKUP_CREATE, DELETE, SYNC, REGISTER)
   - Modified storage server to accept SS_ID and auto-determine role

3. **Replication Features**
   - ✅ **Synchronous replication** - Primary waits for backup ACK
   - ✅ **Automatic** - All CREATE/WRITE/DELETE replicated
   - ✅ **Thread-safe** - Mutex protection for backup operations
   - ✅ **Graceful degradation** - Works even if backup unavailable

### 📝 Key Changes

**New Command-Line Usage:**
```bash
./storage_server <SS_ID> <nm_port> <client_port> <storage_dir>

# Example: Primary server
./storage_server 1 9001 9002 ./storage_data1

# Example: Backup server
./storage_server 2 9003 9004 ./storage_data2
```

### 🔄 How Replication Works

1. **CREATE**: File created on primary → automatically replicated to backup
2. **WRITE**: Changes on primary → entire file synced to backup
3. **DELETE**: File deleted from primary → deleted from backup
4. **Failover**: Name Server redirects clients to backup if primary is down

_**User (2025-11-05 08:25Z)**_

1. Backup Server Connection Establishment

Your first instinct is correct: The Name Server should orchestrate this.

The NM is the "central coordinator". Passing backup info via command-line arguments is too static for a system where servers can "disconnect and reconnect at any time".

Recommended Flow:

    SS-1 (Primary) registers with the NM.

    SS-2 (Backup) registers with the NM.

    The NM now knows about both. It "pairs" them.

    The NM sends a message to SS-1 containing SS-2's IP and port (e.g., its client_port).

    SS-1 can now (and only now) establish its replication connection to SS-2.

2. Backup Server Listening Port

Use the same client_port.

This is a design choice, but using the same port is much simpler and more elegant. Your backup server's main listening loop can accept a new connection, read the first Message, and then decide how to handle it:

    If operation == OP_READ, etc. → Treat it as a client request.

    If operation == OP_REPLICATE_WRITE, etc. → Treat it as a primary server replication.

There is no need to manage a second server socket and thread.

3. File Access Permissions (ADDACCESS)

Yes, these must be replicated.

The goal is to duplicate "every file and folder in an SS". Your access permissions (likely in metadata.txt) are just as important as the file data. If the primary fails and the backup takes over, all access control would be lost if you don't replicate this.

Any operation (like ADDACCESS or REMACCESS) that modifies your persistent metadata file must trigger an asynchronous replication of that metadata file.

4. UNDO Operation

Yes, the result of the UNDO must be replicated.

Don't replicate the UNDO command itself. That's complex. The simplest, most robust solution is:

    Primary server receives UNDO <filename>.

    Primary server copies its undo/<filename>.bak over files/<filename>.

    This file modification is just a standard "write" event.

    This new file content is then asynchronously replicated to the backup server.

The backup server doesn't need its own undo history; it just needs to mirror the primary's current file state.

5. Initial Sync

Yes, an initial sync is mandatory.

The specification says you are "duplicating every file and folder". If the backup only has files created after it started, it's not a duplicate and cannot take over if the primary fails.

When the NM pairs a primary and a new backup (from Q1), the first thing the primary must do is a "bulk sync": it must send all its existing files and metadata to the backup. Only after this is complete should it switch to replicating new writes as they happen.

6. Metadata Replication

Yes. Absolutely.

Your metadata.txt file is data. It must be treated like any other file.

    It must be sent during the "Initial Sync" (Q5).

    Any operation that changes it (ADDACCESS, REMACCESS, CREATE, DELETE) must trigger a replication of the entire updated metadata.txt file to the backup. any doubts- directly ask me

_**User (2025-11-05 08:36Z)**_

1. Name Server Message for Backup Info

Go with Option A: A new dedicated operation.

While Option B (in the ACK) is possible, it's inflexible. A dedicated operation (e.g., OP_NM_BACKUP_INFO) is cleaner for a few reasons:

    Timing: The NM might not have a backup "pair" ready at the exact moment SS1 registers. It might pair SS1 with SS2 (which registers 30 seconds later).

    Re-assignment: If SS2 (the backup) fails, the NM needs a way to tell SS1, "Stop trying to contact SS2; here are the details for SS3, your new backup." You can't do that with a registration ACK.

2. Initial Bulk Sync Trigger

The Primary (SS1) should automatically start the sync after receiving backup info.

This is the most robust "push" model. The Primary is responsible for ensuring its backup is up-to-date.

    NM sends OP_NM_BACKUP_INFO to SS1 (with SS2's details).

    SS1 receives this, connects to SS2.

    SS1 immediately begins the bulk sync.

Yes, you should add a new operation (or set of operations) for this. OP_BACKUP_INIT_SYNC is a good name. This tells the backup server, "A bulk sync is starting; prepare to receive files."

3. Metadata Replication Strategy

Option A: A new operation OP_BACKUP_METADATA.

This is the most efficient and logical method.

    Why: Operations like ADDACCESS or DELETE change only the metadata, not the file contents. It would be very inefficient to re-send a 10GB file just because you added a user.

    Implementation: You would create a dedicated handler. When the Primary SS changes metadata.txt, it sends an OP_BACKUP_METADATA message to the backup, which contains the entire updated metadata.txt content.

4. ADDACCESS/REMACCESS Implementation

Your flow is correct. These handlers belong on the Storage Server.

This is a tricky point in the spec. While the document says the NM "handles" Access Control, this must mean it manages or proxies the request. The persistent storage of metadata (the Access Control Lists) must be on the Storage Server.

Therefore, your flow is the only one that works:

    Client sends ADDACCESS to the NM.

    The NM verifies the request (e.g., checks if the client is the owner) and identifies the correct Primary SS.

    The NM forwards an SS_ADDACCESS command to that Primary SS.

    The Primary SS executes the command, updating its persistent metadata.txt file.

    This change then triggers the Primary SS to send an OP_BACKUP_METADATA (from Q3) to its Backup SS.

Yes, you must add handlers for ADDACCESS and REMACCESS to your storage server.

5. Bulk Sync Implementation Details

Send them one-by-one, and yes, include the entire structure.

The spec requires "duplicating every file and folder in an SS".

    Method: Creating a giant batch operation is very complex (buffering, packet loss, etc.). A simpler and more robust method is to just iterate and send:

        Primary → Backup: OP_BACKUP_INIT_SYNC (to start)

        Primary → Backup: OP_BACKUP_METADATA (sends metadata.txt)

        Primary → Backup: OP_BACKUP_FILE (sends files/file1.txt)

        Primary → Backup: OP_BACKUP_FILE (sends files/file2.txt)

        Primary → Backup: OP_BACKUP_UNDO_FILE (sends undo/file1.bak)

        ...etc.

        Primary → Backup: OP_BACKUP_SYNC_COMPLETE (to finish)

    Content: You must include the files/ directory, the metadata.txt file, and the undo/ directory. If you don't, the backup is not a true duplicate and cannot take over.

6. Backup Server Role After Primary Failure

Yes, the backup becomes the temporary primary, and "match back" is required on recovery.

The document explicitly describes this:

    Failure: The NM detects the Primary has failed.

Takeover: The NM "should be able to retrieve the requested data" from the backup. This means the NM now sends all client requests to the backup. The backup is now "acting as primary."

Recovery: The original Primary (SS1) comes back online and reconnects to the NM.

"Match Back": The NM must now orchestrate a reverse sync. The "duplicated stores should be matched back to the original SS". This means the (acting primary) Backup must sync all changes it received back to the recovered SS1.

    Failover: Once SS1 is fully synced, the NM can designate it as the Primary again, and SS2 returns to being a Backup.

7. Undo Directory Replication

Yes, your entire analysis is correct.

    The undo/ directory must be replicated, as it's part of the SS data.

The undo/ files must be sent during the initial sync (Q5).

Your UNDO flow is perfect: The UNDO operation on the Primary is a write (copying undo/file.bak to files/file). This write is then replicated to the backup just like any other file change. The backup doesn't need to know why the file changed.
